_meta.what_firm_does: Brex is a fintech (corporate cards, spend management, banking). This role sits in Operations: investigating transaction-monitoring and screening alerts for financial crimes, filing SARs, handling sanctions screening escalations, identifying control improvements, supporting vendor integrations.
_meta.jd_wants: 2+ yrs financial risk ops (AML, sanctions, due diligence); knowledge of FinCEN/FINRA/OFAC; data analytics (SQL, Looker, Excel); AI-tool familiarity; cross-functional work with Legal/Eng/Product; strong written/verbal communication; detail-oriented and curious.
_meta.status: DRAFT — STRETCH role: Jamie has data-analysis + ops + detail strengths but NOT a formal AML/TM title. Answers are honest about the gap. Jamie to decide whether to apply.

Why are you interested in this role?: What pulls me to this role is the investigative core of it—looking closely at data, noticing what doesn't add up, and building the controls that catch it. I'll be honest: I haven't held a formal transaction-monitoring title. But the underlying work is what I'm good at. In my consulting work I helped audit 300+ cases and quantify $362K in costs, overturning assumptions people had accepted as fact simply by examining the evidence. That instinct—don't take the surface answer—is exactly what alert review rewards, and I'd love to apply it in a high-stakes financial-crimes context.
Why do you want to work at Brex?: Two reasons. First, I want to grow into the risk and compliance space, and Brex is a serious place to do that—the work genuinely protects the company and its customers. Second, this is a role where my real strengths transfer: careful data analysis, operational discipline, clear documentation, and cross-functional collaboration. My first exposure to finance was at Kronos Research, a quantitative trading firm, so I'm comfortable in fast-paced, detail-intensive environments. I learn new systems and domains quickly, and I'd bring real rigor and curiosity to the team while I build the AML and monitoring expertise the role deepens.
How do you approach learning something n: I treat it like a needs assessment: I figure out what I actually need to know, find the people who know it, and build my understanding deliberately rather than guessing. I use AI tools daily to accelerate that—I've even been building a RAG-based system in my own time that reasons over large bodies of information. With data tools specifically, I'm strong in Excel and analytics and pick up new query and reporting platforms fast. I'd be candid with my manager about where I'm ramping, and I'd close the gap quickly because I'd rather over-prepare than miss something that matters.
Tell us about yourself / anything else: I'm an analytical organizer with a growth mindset, grounded in an MS in Applied Organizational Psychology from USC. My supervisor called me the most organized person he's ever worked with, and I scored 5/5 on punctuality and deadlines—the kind of reliability that matters when you're filing reports and handling escalations. I'm honest about where I'm still building expertise, and equally confident in the strengths I'd bring on day one: data analysis, attention to detail, and a genuine appetite to dig into a problem until it's solved.
